sentences.text: My town, or not?
Rolling.
I was born in Dubno, Poland.
In-- in '20-- 1920-- the 6th of June.
What I remember?
I remember I was born to a-- my-- my mother was married to a widower, to a big family.
I was the youngest in-- in the family.
I had three brothers and two sisters-- besides me and my sister in Israel.
My father died when I was a child of maybe 1 year and a 1/2.
That, I don't remember.
My childhood-- I'm-- I was in a comfortable home-- not especially rich, but it was business.
He had a oil-- a small oil factory, not on electric, but was running still on horses, you know the-- these day.
It wasn't the electric.
It was--
Oil-- what we eat now here-- fish oil.
From-- I don't know how you say.
From-- in this sunflower.
From young-- from this-- grain from sun-- sunflower oil.
And then we had a very big business-- the wheat, you know what?
That was one of the-- a big business; and a small shop, a grocery shop, to supply the people who used to come from the villages for the oil.
They was supply.
He was supplying them.
He had a quite-- I say, is a small shop, but quite-- we had a good living.
Then I went to school.
Sorry.
What happened to the business when your father died?
When my father died, my brothers and my mother did carry on the same way as before.
It was difficult, but we carried on.
We had a-- a Christian man, what he did work, since I've left home, since the-- the change of the government.
Then we didn't have any more.
When The Russians took Poland, we didn't have any more business, you see.
But I remember him from the day what I was born, still I left it.
He was working in this factory from oil.
What else can I tell you?
Then I-- I wasn't a very healthy child.
And that what I had everything, what--
[LAUGHS] --more than everybody else, because of there.
Then I went to a Polish school.
And in Polish school, I went till standard seven.
That was a must.
That was from the governmental school.
Afterwards, for metric-- it was very difficult for Jewish people to come in in a Polish school, because we was accepted four or five every year-- six, you know, like that.
So I went to Krzemiencia.
That is one of--
K-R-Z-E-M-I-E [NON-ENGLISH]-- C-A.
That is in-- that is about 40 or 45 kilometers from Dubno.
And that is a special town for education.
There, you had a lot of private schools and a lot of government schools.
And from-- and nearly from all over, surrounded the towns used to come there for education.
That I spent three years there.
I did stay in the hostel-- in the school hostel.
And one of my sisters was married to there.
That what I make it to come, but I had to be in a hostel, because I wasn't accepted to be in private.
You know, the-- somebody had to be responsible for me.
I was already a big girl, but still, I had to be responsible.
And then I spent three years in-- and I came home.
I wasn't working, because I used to help at home.
And that was my-- my childhood-- a very good childhood I had.
I didn't have a Jewish education, because I went first to the Polish school.
And after that, also I didn't have any Jewish education.
Right?
We came from a orthodox home.
My uncle, and-- and auntie, and my cousins went to Israel in 1928.
It was a long time ago.
It was a long time ago.
And he was the one what he used to always the speeches in-- in-- in synagogues, you know, to make-- to make aware the people what is going to happen-- what is going to happen.
And they was-- they still-- I've got still two cousins from-- from this side in-- in Israel.
My mother was from, also from not a big town.
And she was young.
She was a young person-- a person what always worked.
She used to help people.
Friday were-- you know, Poland was very cold-- very, very cold-- behind 20, sometimes more.
She used Friday-- Friday morning-- call these people-- you know, people what they really didn't have.
They used to come for coffee, and sandwiches, and that was her standing all the winter still, start to be a little bit warmer.
Poor people-- you know, people what they used to buy eggs and chickens and sell, you know, for just the [NON-ENGLISH].
That is the-- the same--
Yeah.
This is the same kind of people.
Did not all.
There was a 10 of them in our district, but they used to always come to us.
And that was the standing quota for winter.
Still-- you know-- still they could manage to-- on their own.
My oldest brother was the governor in shul.
We was-- we was people what-- we wasn't extremely rich or extremely-- but we-- we managed to be in-- in with everybody, like you say, to help one another.
The holidays was beautiful-- in one way beautiful, in one way not, because we didn't have my father.
But it was always Pesach prepared, and made, and-- [LAUGHS]
--four weeks before Pesach, we couldn't touch it-- nothing.
That was washed, and that was-- you can't put your bread.
And you can't put-- you-- you know?
[LAUGHS] The-- not like now.
You going here, you buy everything was made.
Every single thing was made by-- by-- in-- in the home-- the baking, the everything.
And matzah was baking in town.
We didn't get matzah from somewhere.
It's a special place, it was.
And they used to start before Pesach.
For us, the children, for us was a-- a outing to go and see how they baked the matzahs.
Well, it's very difficult-- with machinery and-- it is not only the Jewish people did work.
It was employed a lot of non-Jewish people.
Because to supply-- we-- we were 12,000 Jews in-- in Dubno.
In spare time, I used to help with in shop-- help in there.
I used to go to collect for the blue box.
I belonged to Hashomer Hatzir, to a-- to the movement.
I used to go for-- we have to twice or maybe three times a week to meet.
We done it a lot of interesting outings and a lot of interesting things.
Yes, yes.
Yes, I used to go.
Not far.
We couldn't go far.
There was-- you know-- more or less, we could walk.
It wasn't you-- [LAUGHS] --going in a car, you drive.
You have to walk all over.
Actually, there is a forest.
The most, there is a forest.
Then you could make a fire.
We could eat.
We could dance.
We could do.
I remember, I think, like Lag B'Omer, we used to walk through the town, and the parade was taken by the government.
We used to invite people, you know, to see.
[CRYING]
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Which holidays, you--
Rosh Hashanah, and Yom Kippur, that was a very sad-- not like here.
It's a very sad holiday-- very sad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Absolutely everybody is killed-- everybody.
The two brothers was straight-- straight away.
One brother, my sister, and I, and then my niece, survived in a-- in a field where you bury potatoes.
They survived-- I don't want to-- it's very difficult to say.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She's now arrived in Israel-- my niece.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They survived.
They had a lot of jewelry and gold, you know, gold in money, not gold like-- you know, in rubles.
And she-- they had a lot.
They gave up everything to be saved.
After the war, when--
In a-- yeah.

And they used to-- the-- these one what they took from him-- all the jewelry and everything-- he used-- they used to-- you know, their life, of course, it wasn't safe to go in to spare-- to save Jewish people.
And so they used to bring in the night a little bit of soup, a little bit of water, a little bit something to keep them, you know?
In a hole, until, you know-- we didn't have Frigidaires, you know?
In these days, there wasn't Frigidaires.
It was made holes, and it was very cold, you know?
In-- in winter it was warm.
We used to keep all the potatoes and greens and everything.
It was warm.
And in summer, it was cold.
In these days there wasn't Frigidaires.
You had to adjust yourself to something else, you see?
He-- he survived.
And they all three survived.
How many-- how long did they stay and sit?
The whole war.
They stayed from '41 until the war was finished.
For four years, yeah.
She couldn't walk.
The-- my niece couldn't walk.
She couldn't stretch herself.
But my brother was straightaway mobilized.
He was mobilized.
There was still the fight in Berlin.
You know?
There it was the whole time.
And he was hit very bad hit in a-- in-- in the foot.
And he couldn't work.
From chilblains?
From-- no, no-- the war in Berlin--
--you know, when he was in-- they straight away-- who else-- who survived from the Jewish people wasn't enough.
It was not enough of them-- you sit in a hole or something.
They still mobilized you to go to-- to fight in Berlin.
And then he survived.
And he came home.
We had property, a lot of property-- a lot of property, because his home, my other brother's.
And he wrote to me a letter.
I must come.
We will sell the property.
He sold something of the property.
And they killed him, the-- the people, straight away.
They knew he got money.
So my niece left for Lviv.
This is a big town.
And there she went to school.
And she went to university.
And she finished doctor there in Lviv [NON-ENGLISH].
And you say she had just arrived in Israel?
She just arrived six, seven months, and they not settled.
And they-- you know, they-- I think it was left something, a chip in there, you know, being she only speaks about the father, and she speaks, you know.
She doesn't remember things what I did want to ask her to know.
Because she was there.
And she doesn't remember things.
She doesn't.
And she--
Everybody was killed-- everybody.
My one sister-- this man what he did work by us in the-- for the oil factory-- she had a very, very big about eight-room house, and business, and everything.
And she ask him, I give you everything.
I write it down and sign.
Spare me.
Let me be in the-- somewhere in this-- in the house, too.
He agreed to everything.
And on the-- after four days, he came with the Gestapo.
And they was killed in their house-- she and the-- her husband and the daughter.
I will tell you.
I-- we was living in a street where it was only eight families-- Jewish families.
Because our business couldn't be in center town.
You had to go a little bit further, you see?
So we-- we was like one family there.
You see?
It was only eight families.
And it's the same business-- any-- of all of-- of the eight people who hadn't have-- more or less the same business-- not a oil factory, but wheat, and a little bit of shop, and that.
I feel-- we feel it afterwards.
You know, not in-- still-- still I was in school.
Still the-- but when I finished school, and I couldn't go to high school, then that I feel that I don't belong to them.
No, I-- I want to tell you.
I had the most-- I had non-Jewish-- in the movements, yes, the Jewish-- friends and everything.
But at school and at home, then I had Czechish, two Czechish-- Czechish girls.
Because they-- there's a lot of Czechish people.
Even we had a colony of German people behind us.
Behind our garden was already built in German people-- where they did live over the years.
And I was friends with them, with these two girls.
I was friends the whole time we went to school.
A matter of fact-- and she used to come.
She did stay very far from school.
She used come in the morning and have breakfast with me-- [NON-ENGLISH].
I still remember.
I still now wish I had from-- from them a card in Russia to come back.
And that is the-- that is the story.
Germans arrived when I-- that was-- I went away the day when they arrived.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took straight away 40 people-- 40 men.
And they hanged them straight away.
That was the good-- that was the good morning.
That was the good morning in our town.
It's true.
I knew now-- I-- I remember before, you see, before.
Because I-- I used to write to my brother-- this one what he survived-- to ask him if.
And he told me who, and where, and-- and how it happened-- straightaway.
Straightaways-- it wasn't-- it wasn't waiting for tomorrow, but straight away.
And they was all-- all of them was killed.
It was only these one.
You see, they believe-- we believed so much in the Red Army, as that will-- to take three, four days and it will be all right, you know?
The-- the propaganda was so-- in such a measure.
As every-- everybody did believe.
All right, it start the war, but it will be three days, four days, eight days.
That's what my mother said.
You don't-- don't need nothing.
You just go away for a few days.
That's goodbye.
Germany invaded Poland in '39, and it was-- it was divided.
You know, Poland was divided into 1/2 of Germany-- 1/2 was belonging to Germany and 1/2 to Russia.
Our part of was under Russia's government.
And we-- actually, we had 1 year and a 1/2 quiet.
You see, we couldn't go for passports if you had a business.
We couldn't-- we couldn't say as we needed something.
You know, we didn't live free.
We-- every day it was-- we did hear another story.
These was arrested.
They sent out a lot of people to Siberia-- a matter of fact, as I met them after the war.
Some did survive-- rich, very rich people.
They came in the night and they send them up.
So every day was a new story.
This-- but still, you see, as a-- as a child, it did help me, because I spoke Russian from childhood.
In the home we did speak.
My mother didn't know one Polish word.
She went to a Russian school in-- you know.
Poland was divided three, four times.
It's not only the last time.
They stayed for-- for the year and a 1/2.
But it was times in history, if you remember, three times was Poland divide.
Russia, yes.
We-- we had to speak Russia, because there was only eight families in the street.
And it was--
It was a Russian-speaking area, yes.
What is the name of the town?
I forgot.
Dubno.
Yeah.
It's more to the-- to the Russian borders.
The Polish-Russian borders was in Ostrog.
That is about 50 kilometers from-- from our town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
When Poland invaded, that was say 1 year and a 1/2 we was living.
And then all of a sudden, in the morning, we did hear that is start of war.
And we went away for 10 days.
That was--
We knew.
We knew, because the radios and the news out of-- and the Russian must invade our part of-- where we-- in Dubno, our part.
So we went-- we ran away for 10 days.
That was after Rosh Hashanah, and 10 days to Yom Kippur.
And we went away to a village-- to a-- just the whole family.
We didn't even lock the doors.
We went away.
Everything we-- everybody, we went away.
We stayed for 10 days, and then we hear that's the-- the Red Army is coming.
And they don't touch anybody.
They don't kill.
They don't do.
And we came back home.
We came back.
That was between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
And that this-- they straight away took away a room from us and, you know, other-- other things.
But that was nothing.
That was nothing to compare with what--
That was the Russian army that took over the--
That was the Russian army, because we stayed-- in this street was the Polish army opposite, like-- like here the-- you see, like here the-- the houses.
That was our house, and that was the whole settling from the Polish army.
So the-- the Red Army took it-- hold of this position.
I'm sure you must have felt very safe living with the Red Army on the other side of the road to you.
You know, we don't know safe.
You know, we-- the stories what did go-- they will just take away the house, they will send them to Siberia-- you know, all these stories.
We-- we just lived by year and by the day.
That was the-- the whole story.
And then we did hear what is Hitler doing to the other side in Poland.
And start to come a lot of people from Krakow, from Warsaw, who only can escape came to our part.
They thought that that will be a safe haven.
They will stay here.
They, yes.
We had one doctor, Dr.
Fischler.
He did stay with us.
And where he disappeared, and what happened to him, I can't tell you.
He was a wonderful person-- a doctor, very nice person.
And I think when he did smell out that it's going further-- it will not stay like that-- so he went away-- didn't say-- you see, you didn't even say to your mother where you going.
Everybody was terrified.
You're gone.
That is the-- that was in '39 through '41.
It was earlier.
It-- he went away a few months before-- I remember a few good months before to-- from Poland-- from our home.
And then in '41 started the big war.
What happened?
Sunday-- that was Shavuot.
What you call this in--
Shavuot.
And we came home, me and my brother, and my friends.
We came home joking and-- and--
From town.
We went-- you know, we-- we went out for-- our town had a very big river, Ikva.
We used to go-- summer we used to go with, you know, with boats out-- everybody.
And we came home.
We went to sleep.
And 4 o'clock we heard bombs.
Bombs is going.
Ambulances is running.
And we didn't know what's going on.
And 10 o'clock, Molotov-- that-- that is-- was our minister-- the Russians-- declared us war did start.
We mustn't be scared.
The Red Army will organize.
It is very sad.
They're very sad, because in Kiev, they bombed not only this part where they took from-- from Poland, but they did bomb in Russia already.
Kiev was bombed.
Harka was bombed.
That was going on for three solid days, like a rain-- did come five, six-- go away-- another five, six-- throwing bombs.
Aeroplanes with bombs-- with bombs.
It was impossible to believe-- impossible.
Like a rain-- you know, you-- you lie down on the floor.
Next to you, somebody killed.
You go away.
You don't even care who's killed, what's killed.
You-- it was like in a dream.
You didn't know what's going on.
And then that was a-- the third day-- the third or the fourth-- the fourth day, I think, as they did quiet down with the bombs.
Because their army had to walk in.
The German army has to walk in.
And there was ten of us, more than 10 of us.
And everybody say it will go.
We will sit by the borders-- by the Russian borders.
Maybe if it's something, we will go over.
If not--
No, my mother says, how can you leave everything and go?
We didn't have these-- the sense of what will happen.
Nobody has the sense-- must look after the-- the-- the houses.
After that, I'm not going nowhere.
You must go.
Then I-- we went-- 10-- 10 of us.
My mother say, you're going for three days.
Don't take nothing.
I went out just like that, with a little-- I had a-- not a jersey, but a thin coat, you know, a green-- I remember still I did sell it for-- for a piece of bread.
[LAUGHS] And that was--
Nothing.
I had-- no.
Money I had.
Money I had.
And I had jewelry-- a few jewelries.
That what I had.
That was-- and we went.
It took us a few days.
It took us three, four days till we came there.
And we went to Ostrog.
That is the borders-- the old borders from Poland and Russia.
So when we came there, we straight away did feel that it is not a good time.
The-- the bombing stopped only for the-- for the army, for the German army to walk in.
You know how they did go out on bikes, on big bikes?
And in every bike was arranged to kill you if you going-- you know?
They-- that was the first time.
And then came the heavy army.
That was on the fourth day when they walk into our town.
No.
Lately, no.
I was the one at home.
Everybody was already settled and married, you know?
Yeah, because my-- my sister was already in Israel.
I told you.
She went away in '30.
That's-- my brothers straight-- nobody did move.
And they was killed-- straight away was killed.
And this one ran away to the same village what-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --but we did sit the 10 days.
Money I had.
I don't know why I had money.
I had a lot of rubles, because you couldn't buy nothing for the rubles.
I had-- yes.
My-- I don't know if my mother gave me, because she say, take whatever you can take it, because the rubles we will-- we will throw them away, or we will burn them, because it's nothing you buy.
So I took about 1,000 or 2,000 rubles, and I divide it between these-- what we went, the whole group, because I was scared to carry money.
And I had the-- the few jewelries that would help me out to come over the--
Then afterwards, a few young people came already from Dubno.
And he say, you can't go back.
You can't.
You don't know what's going on.
It's killing and hanging, and shooting, and every-- everybody don't know what to do, where to run.
You can't go back.
So--
On the-- no.
On the borders there came a few young people from Dubno.
How they did escape?
And they saw the army, and they ran.
There's a few of them escaped, you know?
When we came to the border, everybody went through, like I told you the story.
And by me is written as I'm-- my-- my late father was a-- a businessman.
And I don't belong to the Communist party to go-- you know, to go through.
So I gave him some jewelry.
I brought an-- lovely watch.
They?
No.
Then I say, where must I go?
To Hitler?
Where must I go?
They say that is-- you know, madam, you don't speak.
If you've seen the film, that is that.
And then you say like that with the-- like that.
They stay, and you can't even come near to-- to explain and to stay as I'm-- I-- my-- my father's already dead when I was a child of 1 year and a 1/2.
That-- not explanation-- that is, there wasn't explanation.
Yeah.
So-- [LAUGHS] --a capitalist with-- with nothing.
So I-- I went to-- I gave him something.
I say, I've got something nice for you or for your wife-- gave him the watch, and I went over.
And then did started the walking.
Then we walk still--
At-- at outside.
There's no stay.
There's not hotels.
There's not nothing.
Yeah.
